title: Managing water for poultry in winter
subtitle: Keeping drinkers thawed and birds hydrated when temperatures drop below zero.
excerpt: A frozen drinker is the single most common cause of dehydration in backyard flocks. Birds that can't drink stop laying within twenty-four hours. A few practical measures, heated bases, multiple drinkers, twice-daily checks, keep the flock watered through a UK winter without much effort.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens. BHWT (British Hen Welfare Trust) seasonal care guidance. University of Kentucky Cooperative Extension poultry guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: An electric pad or ring placed under a plastic or metal drinker that keeps the water temperature just above freezing in overnight frosts.

content.text: Laying hens need between 500 ml and a litre of water per day depending on weather and feed type. In winter, drinkers can freeze solid overnight and remain frozen well into the morning while hens stand in front of them unable to drink. A day without water stops egg production; two days without water causes serious stress and health problems.

is the most reliable way to prevent freezing in a coop with an electricity supply. The base runs from dusk to dawn and keeps the water just above 0°C. Set it under a standard plastic or galvanised drinker.

Without electricity, use two drinkers in rotation. Fill both in the morning; put one in the run and keep one inside in a frost-free space. At midday, swap them. The birds have access to liquid water through the coldest part of the day without you needing to visit after dark.

Galvanised metal drinkers handle frost better than thin plastic, which can crack when water inside freezes and expands. If you use plastic drinkers, leave them no more than three-quarters full in forecast freezing weather so there's room for the water to expand without cracking the base.

A simple ball drinker (nipple drinker on a bucket) freezes less easily than an open-trough drinker because the water moves as the bird drinks. In a hard frost, even these will freeze solid overnight unless the bucket is insulated or heated.

Check the drinker first when you open the coop, before you look at the eggs. If it's frozen, replace or thaw it before doing anything else. Carrying a flask of warm water in freezing weather lets you thaw a solid drinker in a minute. Do not use boiling water directly on plastic; a barely warm drinker can absorb boiling water poured in and warm to drinkable temperature within thirty seconds.

Ducks and geese need more water than chickens and must be able to submerge their nostrils to clear mucus. A small container that a duck can get her face into is more important than a large trough she can swim in. In hard frost, provide a bucket refilled twice a day rather than a large trough that is mostly frozen.

Place the drinker under a covered section of the run, out of direct rain and away from the overnight cold of an open sky. A tarpaulin or translucent polycarbonate panel over one corner of the run reduces the freeze rate considerably without restricting the birds' space. The covered area also gives the flock somewhere dry to stand and scratch in wet weather.
